macro_segments.title: Introduction and Review of Previous Principles Regarding Gizbar and Meila

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and context for Daf 21
Ravad's expansion of the principle that moving an object is not stealing without giving it to another person—applies beyond gizbar to anyone with right to handle it
Discussion of pikadon (item held for safekeeping) and shlichus yad—moving it with intent to steal requires giving it to someone else
Rambam's ruling based on Tosefta: meila of stealing (taking and giving to another) removes kedusha, but meila of benefiting does not
Discussion of shinui (transformation) as possible act of stealing and Pagam requirements

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Sending an Agent to Bring Hekdesh Money from One Location—Following Instructions vs. Deviating

micro_segments.title: Opening case: owner sends agent to bring money from window or box; agent brings from different location; devarim shebelev einam devarim principle applied
Question of where meila occurs when agent brings money to owner but does not give it to a third party
Distinction between following instructions exactly vs. deviating: if agent brings from wrong location, agent (not owner) is moel

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Sending Incompetent Agents (Deaf-Mute, Imbecile, Minor)—When Meila Attaches

micro_segments.title: Even deaf-mute, imbecile, and minor can serve as shaliach for meila purposes if they follow instructions—conceptual explanation
If incompetent agents do not follow instructions, responsibility falls to the storekeeper when money is spent

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Case of Owner Remembering During Agent's Mission—Shifting Liability

micro_segments.title: Storekeeper becomes moel when he actually spends the money, assuming both owner and agent have remembered

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Analogy of Olives in a Vat and Monkey/Elephant with Eruv—Extending Agency Without Technical Shlichus

micro_segments.title: Gemara's proof from olives in a vat: olives become susceptible to tumah based on owner's will, not direct action
Better proof from eruv: monkey or elephant carrying eruv item counts as valid eruv, showing intent and causal role suffice
Tosafos challenges eruv analogy; clarifies that third party's action is significant but still counts if tied to owner's initiative
Conclusion: meila focuses on removal from hekdesh through owner's initiative, even if executed through incompetent agents

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Case of Owner Remembering During Agent's Mission—Shifting Liability (continued)

micro_segments.title: If owner remembers hekdesh money is being misused before agent gives it to storekeeper, owner is no longer moel
Braita and debate: if only owner remembers but not agent, then agent becomes moel even though unaware
Gemara resolution: mishnah case assumes both owner and agent remembered; highlights problem of making unaware party liable
Mefaresh notes this is a halacha 'suspended by a hair'—nobody is at fault yet someone is moel; possibility of ones not negating meila

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Fractional Amounts and Partial Compliance—Agent Buys Wrong Item or Wrong Quantity

micro_segments.title: Owner gives agent half a coin for lamps, half for wicks; agent spends all on one item or opposite—both half-amounts insufficient for meila
Owner specifies exact stores; agent switches sources—agent becomes moel for both halves since deviating from instructions
Owner gives two coins for esrog; agent buys esrog for one coin and pomegranate for other—both are moel in their respective actions
Rabbi Yehuda disagrees: esrog for one coin is not what was requested (too small/bad quality); not partial compliance but complete non-compliance
Owner gives dinar for shirt; agent buys half-price shirt and coat—similar debate: is half-price shirt still compliance?

macro_segments.title: Gemara: Parsing When Half-Compliance Counts as Agency—Bargain vs. Different Object

micro_segments.title: Gemara infers from Tanna Kamma that if agent buys half an acre when told to buy full acre, agent remains shaliach for that portion
Gemara suggests case involves item on sale: bought for half price counts as full compliance because same quality obtained for less
Rabbi Yehuda's response: if full coin was given, agent should have obtained better item; partial spending negates agency
Braita: Rabbi Yehuda agrees that with kitnis (legumes, divisible items), both are moel when agent buys only half amount
Gemara explores why kitnis is different: divisible items allow partial compliance vs. indivisible items (shirt, esrog) which become different objects at different prices
Rav Papa's resolution: kitnis sold by exact measure, no bargains possible, so Rabbi Yehuda's bargain argument never applies to kitnis

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Money Deposited with Moneychanger—Implicit Permission to Use

micro_segments.title: If hekdesh money given to moneychanger unbound, moneychanger has implicit right to use it; becomes moel only when spending it, not taking it
If money is bound in a bundle, moneychanger has no right to use it; becomes moel immediately upon spending
Rambam's position: even unbound money to ordinary homeowner (not moneychanger) gives no implicit permission; homeowner becomes moel upon spending
Debate: is storekeeper like moneychanger (Rabbi Yehuda) with implicit permission, or like homeowner (Rabbi Meir) without it?

macro_segments.title: Mishnah and Gemara: Mixed Coins—First vs. Last When Hekdesh Coin Drops into Purse

micro_segments.title: If you declare 'one coin in my purse is hekdesh' without specifying which: Rabbi Akiva says first coin withdrawn is moel; Chachamim say only after all spent
Tosefta explains Rabbi Akiva: not outright meila but safek meila requiring asham taluy (conditional guilt offering)
Tosafos questions why hekdesh coin is not batel berov (nullified in majority); answers: coin is davar chashuv or two-coin scenario only
Distinction: bitel applies to status/issur, not ownership; hekdesh retains ownership even if mixed and identity unknown
If you explicitly say 'a coin from this purse is hekdesh' (not 'in'), language means last coin; Rabbi Akiva agrees with Chachamim in this variant
Gemara debates how to derive 'last coin' interpretation: Rav Pappa brings parallel from terumah/maaser case with breira principle
Gemara resolves: explicit language ('will not be free from hekdesh') indicates last coin; without such stipulation, meaning is unclear

topical_tags.term: Gizbar (treasurer/one with right to handle hekdesh)
Devarim shebelev einam devarim (mental intent is irrelevant)
Cheresh, shoteh, vekatan (deaf-mute, imbecile, minor—incompetent parties)
Ravad (Rabbi Abraham ben David—commentator disagreeing with Rambam)
Rabbi Yehuda (Sage disagreeing with Tanna Kamma on compliance)
Davar chashuv (significant/valuable item not subject to bitel)
